quiz.title: SAT Mathematics: Unit 9 Quiz 10
quiz.subtitle: Evaluating Statistical Claims
quiz.subject: SAT Preparation
quiz.topic: Assessing study design, sampling, and conclusions

questions.question: A researcher wants to survey city residents about a proposed new park. Which of the following sampling methods is most likely to provide a representative sample of the city's population?
options: Surveying shoppers at a local sporting goods store.
Surveying individuals from the city's property tax records.
Surveying individuals from a list of registered voters.
Surveying addresses from a comprehensive residential database.
questions.explanation: To be representative of the entire city, every resident should have an equal chance of being selected. Options A, B, and C introduce selection bias by targeting specific subgroups. Option D uses a comprehensive address list, minimizing bias and ensuring broad coverage.

questions.question: A study was conducted to determine the effect of a new fertilizer on corn yield. 100 plots of land were randomly assigned to either receive the new fertilizer or a standard fertilizer. The plots receiving the new fertilizer produced $15\%$ more corn on average. Which of the following is the most appropriate conclusion?
options: The new fertilizer caused an increase in corn yield for the studied plots.
The new fertilizer guarantees a yield increase for any corn crop.
The new fertilizer is the only factor that influences corn yield.
The results apply to all agricultural crops, not just corn.
questions.explanation: Because the plots were randomly assigned to treatment and control groups, a causal relationship can be inferred for the subjects in the study. However, results cannot be broadly generalized to 'any crop' or interpreted as a universal guarantee for all farmers.

questions.question: A polling organization conducted a survey of 1,000 randomly selected adults in a country to estimate the proportion of adults who support a new law. The survey found that $54\%$ of the respondents support the law, with a margin of error of $3\%$. Which of the following is a correct interpretation of this margin of error?
options: The true proportion of supporters is exactly $54\%$ of the population.
It is plausible that the true population proportion falls between $51\%$ and $57\%$.
Repeating the survey will yield a $54\%$ result in $97\%$ of trials.
Approximately $3\%$ of respondents provided factually incorrect answers.
questions.explanation: A margin of error provides a range within which the true population parameter is likely to fall. Given a $54\%$ result and a $3\%$ margin of error, the plausible interval is $54\% \pm 3\%$, or $51\%$ to $57\%$.

questions.question: A scientist wants to investigate whether drinking green tea lowers blood pressure. The scientist finds 50 people who regularly drink green tea and 50 people who never drink green tea. The study finds that the green tea drinkers have lower blood pressure on average. Why can the scientist NOT conclude that green tea causes lower blood pressure?
options: The sample size of 100 participants is too small for analysis.
The two groups contained different numbers of participants.
The study design was observational rather than randomized.
The study failed to calculate a statistical margin of error.
questions.explanation: In an observational study where participants self-select into groups, confounding variables (such as diet, genetics, or exercise) might cause the observed effect. Cause-and-effect can only be established through a randomized controlled experiment.

questions.question: An online news site asks its readers to click 'Yes' or 'No' on a poll regarding a controversial new policy. Of the 5,000 people who responded, $85\%$ clicked 'No.' Which of the following statements best explains why this result may not represent the opinions of the general public?
options: A sample size of 5,000 is statistically insufficient for accuracy.
The survey question was phrased to force a negative response.
The poll methodology failed to include a calculated margin of error.
The survey relied on voluntary response, attracting opinionated individuals.
questions.explanation: Voluntary response samples are highly susceptible to bias because people who feel strongly about a topic are more likely to participate, while indifferent individuals opt out. This self-selection makes the sample unrepresentative of the general public.

questions.question: A researcher conducted a survey to determine the average number of hours high school students in a specific state spend on homework each night. The researcher surveyed 500 students from a single high-performing private school. Which of the following is the most significant limitation of this study?
options: The sample is not representative of all high school students in the state.
The sample size of 500 is statistically too small for accurate estimation.
The students were not randomly assigned to different homework levels.
The researcher failed to calculate a margin of error for the findings.
questions.explanation: Because the sample was drawn exclusively from one high-performing private school, the results cannot be generalized to the entire state's population of high school students, whose academic environments may differ significantly.

questions.question: A randomized experiment was conducted to compare two different study methods, Method A and Method B. 100 volunteers were randomly assigned to use one of the two methods. Those using Method A had a mean test score of $85$, and those using Method B had a mean test score of $80$. The results were found to be statistically significant. Which of the following is the most appropriate conclusion?
options: Method A guarantees higher scores for any student in any context.
Method A caused the higher mean test score for the study participants.
Method A is exactly $5\%$ more effective than Method B universally.
The sample size of 100 invalidates the statistical significance.
questions.explanation: In a randomized experiment, 'statistically significant' means the observed difference is unlikely due to random chance. Therefore, we can reasonably conclude that the treatment (Method A) likely caused the performance difference within the studied group.

questions.question: A city council wants to estimate the proportion of residents who support a new tax. They mail a survey to every household in the city. Of the 10,000 surveys mailed, 500 were returned, and 400 of those expressed support. Which of the following is a potential source of bias in this study?
options: The sample size of 500 is excessively large for accurate estimates.
Selection bias occurred because every household received a survey.
Nonresponse bias, as respondents may differ systematically from non-respondents.
Measurement bias, because mail surveys are less accurate than phone calls.
questions.explanation: Nonresponse bias occurs when individuals who choose to respond differ systematically from those who do not. With only a $5\%$ return rate, the results likely reflect only those with strong opinions about the tax, skewing the estimate.

questions.question: A study of 2,000 randomly selected adults in a certain country found that $30\%$ of the participants exercise for at least 30 minutes a day. The margin of error for this estimate is $2.5\%$. Which of the following is the most appropriate claim based on this study?
options: Exactly $30\%$ of all adults in the country exercise daily for 30 minutes.
At most $32.5\%$ of all adults in the country meet the daily exercise guideline.
Future surveys of 2,000 adults will always yield exactly $30\%$.
Between $27.5\%$ and $32.5\%$ of all adults in the country likely exercise daily.
questions.explanation: The margin of error creates a confidence interval around the sample statistic. $30\% \pm 2.5\%$ yields a range of $27.5\%$ to $32.5\%$, indicating where the true population proportion is most likely to reside.

questions.question: An environmental group wants to estimate the average lead level in the soil of a city's public parks. They divide the city into 10 geographic regions and randomly select one park from each region to test. Which of the following best describes this sampling method?
options: Convenience sampling based on park accessibility.
Stratified random sampling using geographic regions.
Voluntary response sampling from local park visitors.
Systematic sampling using a fixed numerical interval.
questions.explanation: Stratified random sampling involves dividing the population into distinct subgroups (strata)—here, geographic regions—and then randomly selecting samples from each group. This ensures every region is proportionally represented.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent work! You can confidently evaluate study designs, identify sampling biases, interpret margins of error, and distinguish between correlation and causation.
scoreMessages.70: Good job! Review missed questions and focus on recognizing when randomized assignment allows causal claims versus when observational designs only show associations.
scoreMessages.50: A fair start. Remember: voluntary response and nonresponse are major sources of bias, stratified sampling divides populations into groups, and margin of error creates a plausible range, not a guarantee.
scoreMessages.0: Keep practicing! Start by identifying the population, sampling method, and study design (observational vs. experimental) in every problem. These three elements dictate what conclusions are valid.
